angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.
talkingPoints: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.

The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.

The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.
Add one diligence workstream focused exclusively on decision rights, escalation patterns, and information flow — not as compliance check, but as operating model architecture assessment.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

angle.headline: Why Your QoE Takes Two Days Before Analysis Even Starts
angle.thesis: Middle-market deals fail not because the books are bad, but because the data preparation work that precedes every quality-of-earnings engagement is invisible to the buyer, the seller, and the owner—and AI just made it obsolete.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners with $5M–$50M enterprise value preparing for a sale, acquisition, or institutional capital raise within 12–24 months
angle.relevanceToAudience: If you are building toward an exit or growth capital event in the US-LATAM corridor, the 8–15 hours of manual data preparation that every advisor bills before analysis begins is a tax you pay for operating without standardized financial infrastructure—and it signals to buyers that your business runs on the owner, not the system.
talkingPoints: Every middle-market QoE engagement begins with 8–15 hours of unpaid work normalizing QuickBooks exports, reconciling bank statements, and mapping inconsistent account names—work that produces zero analytical insight.
This data preparation tax exists because the business was built on relationship and instinct, not on financial systems designed to be legible to external capital.

AI is now eliminating this work in 90 minutes, but the real insight is not the speed—it is that the need for manual normalization was always a signal that the Operating Model OS was not built for scale.
The action is not to buy the AI tool; it is to audit whether your financial infrastructure can answer a buyer's question in real time without the controller spending two days preparing a deck.

peopleAngle.challenge: The controller or CFO must shift from being the person who 'knows where everything is' to being the architect of systems that make the business legible without their interpretation.
